stem:
The following text is from a short article about urban wildlife.

Coyotes have quietly become residents of nearly every major North American city. Unlike raccoons or pigeons, they rarely draw attention, slipping through parks and alleys after dark. Biologists estimate that thousands now live within Chicago's city limits alone. Most residents never notice them.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To warn readers about the dangers coyotes pose to city pets.
To inform readers that coyotes have become common but largely unnoticed urban residents.
To compare coyotes with other urban animals like raccoons and pigeons.
To argue that cities should do more to manage coyote populations.

explanation: The text states that coyotes live in cities in large numbers but go unnoticed — that is the informational point, not a warning, comparison, or argument.

stem:
The following text is from a biography of the astronomer Caroline Herschel.

Caroline Herschel is often remembered only as the sister and assistant of the more famous William Herschel. But between 1786 and 1797, she discovered eight comets on her own and compiled a catalog correcting errors in the standard star index of her day. The Royal Astronomical Society later awarded her its Gold Medal.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To describe the working relationship between Caroline and William Herschel.
To explain how comets were discovered in the eighteenth century.
To establish Caroline Herschel's independent scientific achievements.
To criticize the Royal Astronomical Society for ignoring women scientists.

explanation: The text pushes back against the view of Herschel as merely an assistant by listing her own discoveries and recognition.

stem:
The following text is from a magazine article about food science.

Baking a cake might look like art, but it is really chemistry in slow motion. Baking soda releases carbon dioxide when it meets an acid, lifting the batter. Eggs trap air and set the structure. Sugar does far more than sweeten — it tenderizes, browns, and retains moisture.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To provide a recipe for a basic cake.
To argue that baking is more difficult than cooking.
To explain the chemical roles of common baking ingredients.
To trace the history of baking soda as a leavening agent.

explanation: Each sentence after the topic sentence assigns a chemical role to an ingredient — that is the function of the passage.

stem:
The following text is from a travel essay about Iceland.

Even for a country used to geothermal wonders, the Blue Lagoon is unusual. Its milky, mineral-rich water is not a natural hot spring but a byproduct of a nearby power plant, which pumps superheated fluid up from volcanic rock. The plant generates electricity first; the leftover warm water filled a lava field and, eventually, a famous spa.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To describe the mineral content of the Blue Lagoon's water.
To promote the Blue Lagoon as a tourist destination.
To explain that the Blue Lagoon is an accidental byproduct of a power plant.
To compare Iceland's hot springs to those of other countries.

explanation: The passage highlights that what looks natural is actually industrial runoff — the surprising origin is the main point.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a short piece on linguistics.

Most English speakers treat the word 'literally' as an intensifier — 'I literally died' — even when nothing literal happened. Language purists protest, but this shift is neither new nor unusual. Words like 'very,' 'really,' and 'truly' took the same path centuries ago, drifting from factual claim to emphasis.

Which choice best describes the function of the second sentence?

choices.text: It introduces a new definition of the word 'literally.'
It argues that purists have a valid complaint about the word 'literally.'
It acknowledges criticism of the shift and signals that the passage will defend the shift as normal.
It provides an example of a recent change in English grammar.

explanation: The sentence names the objection ('purists protest') and then dismisses it as 'neither new nor unusual,' setting up the historical defense that follows.

stem:
The following text is from an article about sleep research.

For decades, researchers assumed that teenagers who stayed up late were simply undisciplined. More recent work suggests otherwise: during puberty, the body's internal clock shifts by roughly two hours, pushing sleep later. A 7 a.m. school bell, then, rings at what the teenage brain treats as 5 a.m.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To replace a behavioral explanation for teen sleep habits with a biological one.
To recommend that all schools start classes after 9 a.m.
To describe the health consequences of chronic sleep deprivation.
To explain how the human circadian rhythm works in general.

explanation: The passage sets up an old assumption ('undisciplined') and then replaces it with a biological one ('the body's internal clock shifts'). That swap is the function.

stem:
The following text is from an article about materials science.

Gecko feet have inspired a new kind of adhesive. Instead of relying on glue, the material uses millions of microscopic hairs that cling to surfaces through weak molecular forces. The result is a tape that sticks strongly when pressed but releases cleanly when peeled — no residue, no damage.

Which choice best describes the function of the last sentence?

choices.text: It introduces a limitation of the new material.
It compares gecko feet to ordinary tape.
It lists the practical advantages of the gecko-inspired adhesive.
It defines what 'molecular forces' means in physics.

explanation: The final sentence enumerates what the adhesive does well ('sticks strongly,' 'releases cleanly,' 'no residue, no damage') — these are its advantages.

stem:
The following text is from an article about museum conservation.

For more than a century, art conservators cleaned oil paintings with organic solvents, which dissolved grime but sometimes stripped the paint itself. In 2020, a team at the Getty Conservation Institute introduced microemulsions — tiny, water-based droplets that lift dirt without attacking the underlying layers. Early tests on damaged seventeenth-century canvases showed virtually no pigment loss. Several major museums have since adopted the method.

Which choice best describes the structure of the text?

choices.text: It describes a longstanding problem, presents a recent solution, and notes its adoption.
It chronicles the history of the Getty Conservation Institute.
It compares two competing theories of art restoration.
It argues that older cleaning methods should never have been used.

explanation: Sentence 1 identifies the old problem, sentences 2–3 introduce the new microemulsion fix, and the final sentence reports uptake. That is a problem–solution–adoption arc.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a 2023 article by marine biologist Yuki Tanaka about octopus cognition.

Laboratory octopuses have been shown to unscrew jars, navigate mazes, and recognize individual human caretakers. These feats are often cited as evidence of general intelligence. Yet each task also admits a simpler explanation: persistent trial-and-error, scent-tracking, or associative learning tied to feeding schedules. Until experiments can rule out these alternatives, claims about octopus 'insight' should be treated cautiously.

Which choice best describes the function of the third sentence in the text as a whole?

choices.text: It provides alternative, less complex explanations that complicate the interpretation in the previous sentence.
It offers additional examples of octopus problem-solving abilities.
It restates the main argument of the passage in different words.
It explains why laboratory conditions are poor settings for studying octopus behavior.

explanation: Sentence 2 offers the 'intelligence' reading; sentence 3 introduces simpler alternatives ('trial-and-error, scent-tracking, associative learning') that undercut that reading.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a short essay on jazz history.

Bebop, the style pioneered by Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie in the 1940s, is often described as a musical revolution. The description fits the sound — the tempos were faster, the harmonies denser, the melodies more angular than anything in swing. But in another sense it is misleading: bebop musicians themselves saw their work as an extension of the swing tradition, not a rejection of it. Many had played in big bands and continued to borrow phrases and progressions from them.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To argue that bebop was not a revolutionary style at all.
To describe the technical features that made bebop sound different from swing.
To criticize Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie for overstating their originality.
To qualify the common description of bebop as a revolution by showing its continuity with swing.

explanation: The passage accepts that 'revolution' fits the sound but notes the musicians saw continuity — it qualifies the label rather than rejecting it outright.

stem:
The following text is from an article on environmental policy.

Bogotá, Colombia, did not set out to become a model for transit planners. In 2000, the city was so congested that average car speeds fell below those of bicycles. Desperate, officials built a network of dedicated bus lanes — the TransMilenio — that moved more riders per hour than most subway lines at a fraction of the cost. Within a decade, cities from Jakarta to Johannesburg were copying the design.

Which choice best describes the function of the second sentence?

choices.text: It introduces the main argument of the passage.
It criticizes Bogotá's failure to build a subway system.
It compares cars and bicycles as modes of urban transport.
It establishes the severity of the problem that motivated the TransMilenio.

explanation: The 'cars slower than bicycles' detail shows just how bad congestion was, motivating the 'desperate' solution in the next sentence.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a book review.

In her new history of the Dust Bowl, Priya Ramesh does not dwell on the famous photographs of abandoned farms and walls of dust. She assumes readers have seen them. Instead, she reconstructs the decisions of federal agronomists in the 1920s who promoted deep plowing across the southern plains, turning fragile grassland into a ticking bomb. The result is a book less about disaster than about the bureaucratic choices that made disaster inevitable.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To explain how Ramesh's book shifts focus from the disaster itself to the policy choices that caused it.
To criticize Ramesh for omitting the iconic Dust Bowl photographs.
To summarize the history of federal agriculture policy in the 1920s.
To argue that the Dust Bowl was preventable.

explanation: The passage contrasts what Ramesh skips (familiar images) with what she emphasizes (agronomists' decisions), explaining the book's angle.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a magazine feature on neuroscience.

When a person recalls a vivid memory, they often feel they are replaying a recording. But memory researchers have long known that recall is reconstructive: each time a memory is retrieved, it is subtly rewritten. Small details drift. Feelings from the present seep into the past. A memory pulled up a hundred times is less like a DVD and more like a photocopy of a photocopy.

Which choice best describes the function of the underlined analogy 'a photocopy of a photocopy'?

choices.text: It makes the concept of reconstructive memory concrete by comparing it to a familiar process of cumulative degradation.
It suggests that memories become more accurate with repeated retrieval.
It introduces a new scientific theory of how memories are stored in the brain.
It argues that photographs are a better record of events than memory.

explanation: A 'photocopy of a photocopy' gets worse each time — the analogy makes the abstract idea of reconstructive drift vivid and familiar.

stem:
The following text is from an article about competitive chess.

At the 2023 World Rapid Championship, grandmaster Ana Varga drew a position that engines had long considered a clear win for her opponent. Afterward, she explained that she had deliberately steered toward a line where the winning plan required roughly twenty precise moves in a row — moves easy for a computer but punishing for a human under time pressure. The draw was not luck. It was an exploitation of the gap between engine evaluation and human performance.

Which choice best describes the function of the final sentence?

choices.text: It reframes the outcome as a deliberate strategy rather than a fortunate accident.
It provides statistical evidence that human players outperform engines under time pressure.
It introduces Varga's upcoming match against a chess computer.
It criticizes chess engines for overestimating winning positions.

explanation: 'The draw was not luck' directly rejects the lucky reading and relabels it as exploitation of the human–engine gap — a reframing.

stem:
The following text is from an article about ecology.

Sea otters eat enormous quantities of sea urchins. When otter populations collapse, urchin numbers explode, and urchins in turn graze kelp forests down to bare rock. With the kelp gone, dozens of fish and invertebrate species lose their habitat. Protecting otters, then, is not just about otters.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To show that sea otters function as a keystone species whose presence protects an entire ecosystem.
To describe the diet of sea otters.
To explain how kelp forests grow.
To argue that sea urchins should be removed from coastal waters.

explanation: The passage traces otters → urchins → kelp → many species and closes with 'not just about otters,' making the keystone role the point.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a 2022 essay on public libraries.

Critics periodically announce that public libraries have outlived their usefulness in an age of smartphones. The libraries themselves tell a different story. Visits to U.S. public libraries rose through most of the 2010s, and the services residents request most often — tax help, internet access, job-search support, climate-controlled shelter during heat waves — have nothing to do with borrowing books. The building, not the collection, has become the point.

Which choice best describes the function of the final sentence?

choices.text: It introduces a counterargument that libraries are no longer relevant.
It crystallizes the passage's point by reframing what a library primarily provides.
It offers a statistical prediction about future library usage.
It criticizes libraries for spending too little on their book collections.

explanation: After listing non-book services, the final sentence distills the whole argument into one reframing: value comes from the building, not the books.

stem:
The following text is adapted from Kazuo Ishiguro's novel 'The Remains of the Day' (1989). The narrator, Stevens, is an English butler recalling his long career.

It is sometimes said that butlers only truly exist in England. Other countries, whatever title is used, have only manservants. I tend to believe this is true. Continentals are unable to be butlers because they are as a breed incapable of the emotional restraint which only the English race are capable of. Continentals — and by and large the Celts, as you will no doubt agree — are as a rule unable to control themselves in moments of strong emotion, and are thus unable to maintain a professional demeanour.

Which choice best describes the function of the passage within the larger novel?

choices.text: It reveals Stevens's self-important and narrow worldview through the very confidence of his sweeping generalizations.
It provides a neutral historical account of differences between English and continental European service traditions.
It argues that emotional restraint is a measurable professional skill.
It criticizes English butlers for looking down on their continental counterparts.

explanation: The passage's function is characterization: Stevens's unexamined chauvinism ('incapable,' 'as a breed,' 'as a rule') exposes his worldview to the reader, which the novel treats critically.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a 2024 research report on honeybee navigation.

Honeybees were long thought to rely almost entirely on sunlight polarization to navigate. A new study complicates that picture. Researchers tracked foragers on overcast days when polarization cues were unavailable and found that the bees still returned to the hive along nearly straight paths. Analysis of their flight data suggested they were integrating landmarks and wind direction in place of sky cues. Still, the authors caution that their sample was drawn from a single colony in a single season.

Which choice best describes the function of the final sentence in the text as a whole?

choices.text: It tempers the earlier finding by flagging a limitation that keeps the result from being generalized.
It strengthens the study's conclusions by naming the control group used in the experiment.
It provides additional examples of how honeybees use polarization to navigate.
It introduces a new and unrelated line of research on honeybee communication.

explanation: 'Still' signals a pullback: after reporting the finding, the authors caution that a single colony in a single season limits how far it can be generalized.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a 2023 essay on architectural history.

The glass curtain wall is usually credited to twentieth-century modernists like Mies van der Rohe, whose 1958 Seagram Building became its textbook example. The story is tidy but incomplete. Nearly sixty years earlier, in 1903, Willis Polk's Hallidie Building in San Francisco already hung a full glass facade from a steel frame, achieving the same decoupling of wall and structure that modernists would later claim as their own innovation. What modernism added was not the technique but a philosophy that made the technique seem obligatory.

What is the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To reassign credit for the curtain wall from modernists to an earlier building, while clarifying what modernism did contribute.
To argue that the Seagram Building is overrated compared to the Hallidie Building.
To trace the development of steel-frame construction from 1903 to 1958.
To praise Mies van der Rohe for popularizing a technique invented by Willis Polk.

explanation: The passage corrects the attribution ('incomplete,' then the 1903 counterexample) but also concedes modernism's real contribution (the philosophy) — both moves together are the purpose.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a 2024 article on paleontology.

For more than a century, the small Triassic reptile Megalancosaurus was classified as a tree-dwelling glider, largely on the strength of its hooked claws and elongated fingers. A 2024 reanalysis of its shoulder girdle, however, found the joint geometry incompatible with the overhead limb postures that gliding would require. The authors propose instead that Megalancosaurus was a slow arboreal climber, much like a modern chameleon. They concede, though, that the new reading rests on a single partial specimen and that a more complete skeleton could easily overturn it.

Which choice best describes the function of the third sentence in the text?

choices.text: It restates the traditional gliding interpretation of Megalancosaurus in more technical language.
It acknowledges that the 2024 reanalysis produced no new conclusions.
It argues that fossil evidence cannot reliably reveal behavior in extinct reptiles.
It pivots from dismantling the old interpretation to offering a specific replacement hypothesis.

explanation: Sentence 2 rules out gliding; sentence 3 introduces a positive alternative ('slow arboreal climber, much like a modern chameleon'). That pivot from teardown to replacement is the function.
